<skills>
Here is a list of skills that contain domain specific knowledge on a variety of topics.
Each skill comes with a description of the topic and a file path that contains the detailed instructions.
When a user asks you to perform a task that falls within the domain of a skill, use the #tool:readFile tool to acquire the full instructions from the file URI.
<skill>
<name>omarchy</name>
<description>REQUIRED for end-user customization of Linux desktop, window manager, or system config. Use when editing ~/.config/hypr/, ~/.config/waybar/, ~/.config/walker/, ~/.config/alacritty/, ~/.config/kitty/, ~/.config/ghostty/, ~/.config/mako/, or ~/.config/omarchy/. Triggers: Hyprland, window rules, animations, keybindings, monitors, gaps, borders, blur, opacity, waybar, walker, terminal config, themes, wallpaper, night light, idle, lock screen, screenshots, layer rules, workspace settings, display config, and user-facing omarchy commands. Excludes Omarchy source development in ~/.local/share/omarchy/ and omarchy-dev-* workflows.
</description>
<file>/home/bap/.claude/skills/omarchy/SKILL.md</file>
</skill>
<skill>
<name>agent-customization</name>
<description>**WORKFLOW SKILL** — Create, update, review, fix, or debug VS Code agent customization files (.instructions.md, .prompt.md, .agent.md, SKILL.md, copilot-instructions.md, AGENTS.md). USE FOR: saving coding preferences; troubleshooting why instructions/skills/agents are ignored or not invoked; configuring applyTo patterns; defining tool restrictions; creating custom agent modes or specialized workflows; packaging domain knowledge; fixing YAML frontmatter syntax. DO NOT USE FOR: general coding questions (use default agent); runtime debugging or error diagnosis; MCP server configuration (use MCP docs directly); VS Code extension development. INVOKES: file system tools (read/write customization files), ask-questions tool (interview user for requirements), subagents for codebase exploration. FOR SINGLE OPERATIONS: For quick YAML frontmatter fixes or creating a single file from a known pattern, edit the file directly — no skill needed.</description>
<file>copilot-skill:/agent-customization/SKILL.md</file>
</skill>
</skills>


<agents>
Here is a list of agents that can be used when running a subagent.
Each agent has optionally a description with the agent's purpose and expertise. When asked to run a subagent, choose the most appropriate agent from this list.
Use the #tool:runSubagent tool with the agent name to run the subagent.
<agent>
<name>Explore</name>
<description>Fast read-only codebase exploration and Q&A subagent. Prefer over manually chaining multiple search and file-reading operations to avoid cluttering the main conversation. Safe to call in parallel. Specify thoroughness: quick, medium, or thorough.</description>
<argumentHint>Describe WHAT you're looking for and desired thoroughness (quick/medium/thorough)</argumentHint>
</agent>
</agents>

v.invocationMessage: Explore the dotfiles workspace to understand its structure and common patterns

toolSpecificData.description: Explore the dotfiles workspace to understand its structure and common patterns
toolSpecificData.prompt: Thoroughly explore the dotfiles workspace to understand its purpose, structure, and common configuration patterns. Identify the main technologies and tools used (e.g., window managers, shells, terminals). Look for any existing documentation or README files. Summarize the key components and how they are organized.
toolSpecificData.result:
Based on my exploration of the dotfiles workspace, here's a comprehensive summary:

## **Workspace Purpose**
This is a **Linux desktop configuration repository** for a fully customized Wayland-based development environment running on **CachyOS** (Arch-based distribution). The dotfiles follow the **XDG Base Directory Specification** with `.config/` subdirectories for each application.

---

## **Core Architecture**

### **Display & Desktop Environment**
- **Window Manager**: **Hyprland** (Wayland compositor)
- **Session Manager**: UWSM (Universal Wayland Session Manager) - auto-started at TTY1
- **Theme Manager**: **Omarchy** (custom theming system) - manages themes across multiple applications
- **Desktop**: CachyOS (distribution-specific configurations)

### **Shells & Terminals**
- **Primary Shell Options**:
  - **Fish Shell** - Advanced configuration with completions, functions, plugins, and custom themes
  - **Zsh Shell** - Using Oh-My-Zsh framework with `xiong-chiamiov-plus` theme
  - Plugins: `git`, `dnf`, `zsh-autosuggestions`, `zsh-syntax-highlighting`

- **Terminal Emulator**: **Alacritty**
  - Uses JetBrainsMono Nerd Font (size 14)
  - Integrates Omarchy theme system for consistent styling
  - Configured with 14px padding and borderless decorations

### **Code Editors**
- **Neovim**: LazyVim starter configuration (modern Lua-based setup)
  - Uses lazy package manager (`lazy-lock.json`)
  - Plugin system with custom configuration in `lua/` directory
  - Includes LSP support and extensive tooling

- **VS Code**: Full configuration stored (user settings, extensions, MCP servers)

---

## **Tools & Utilities**

| Category | Tools |
|----------|-------|
| **System Monitoring** | btop (system monitor) |
| **Media/Audio** | cava (audio visualization with Omarchy theming) |
| **Input Methods** | fcitx, fcitx5 (CJK language support) |
| **Font Config** | Custom fontconfig (fonts.conf) |
| **Application Menu** | elephant + walker (with Lua-based custom menus) |
| **System Info** | fastfetch (with Omarchy version/theme display) |
| **PDF Viewer** | evince (with custom settings) |
| **Autostart Apps** | walker (launcher), arch-update-tray |

---

## **Configuration Patterns & Structure**

### **Directory Organization**
```
dotfiles/
├── fish/                 # Fish shell config
├── zsh/                  # Zsh config (.zshrc)
├── nvim/                 # Neovim (LazyVim)
├── alacritty/            # Terminal emulator
├── btop/                 # System monitor theme
├── cava/                 # Audio visualizer
├── fcitx/ & fcitx5/      # Input methods
├── elephant/             # Menu system + Omarchy integrations
├── fastfetch/            # System info display
├── fontconfig/           # Font configuration
├── dconf/                # GNOME dconf settings (user profiles)
├── evince/               # PDF viewer settings
├── cachyos/              # CachyOS-specific configs
├── Code/                 # VS Code configuration
├── environment.d/        # Environment variables
└── autostart/            # Desktop autostart entries
```

### **Theme Integration Pattern**
The setup heavily integrates **Omarchy** theming:
- Terminal (Alacritty) imports theme from `~/.config/omarchy/current/theme/alacritty.toml`
- Cava uses `theme = 'omarchy'`
- Elephant includes Lua menus for dynamic Omarchy theme switching
- Fastfetch displays current Omarchy version and theme

---

## **Key Technologies & Frameworks**
- **Wayland Protocol** (modern display server)
- **Hyprland** (Wayland compositor with animations, workspaces, window rules)
- **LazyVim** (modern Neovim distribution)
- **Oh-My-Zsh** (Zsh plugin framework)
- **XDG Base Directory Spec** (standard Linux config locations)
- **TOML/JSON/Lua** configuration formats

---

## **No Central README**
The workspace has **no top-level README or main documentation file** - configuration is organized by tool/application rather than having a central guide. Individual components (like Neovim) have their own README entries, but the overall setup documentation is implicit in the structure.

This appears to be a **highly customized, theme-focused development setup** emphasizing visual consistency, modern technologies (Wayland, Hyprland), and internationalization (fcitx5) across all system components.

edits.text: description: Workspace instructions for dotfiles repository, focusing on fish/zsh shell configs and Hyprland window manager configs.
## Shell Configuration (Fish/Zsh)
When editing or creating fish or zsh configuration files:
- Ensure proper shell syntax and follow best practices for shell scripting.
- Consider cross-compatibility if configurations need to work in both fish and zsh environments.
- Use appropriate quoting and escaping for variables and commands.
## Hyprland Configuration
When working with Hyprland configuration files:
- Be mindful of Hyprland's configuration syntax, including window rules, keybindings, and animations.
- Consider performance implications when adding complex animations or rules.
- Ensure configurations align with Wayland standards.
## General Dotfiles Practices
- Follow XDG Base Directory Specification for configuration file locations.
- Use the omarchy skill for theming-related customizations in applications like alacritty, cava, and other themed components.
- Maintain consistency across related configurations for a cohesive desktop experience.

<attachment id="prompt:create-instructions.prompt.md" filePath="/home/bap/.vscode/extensions/github.copilot-chat-0.38.2/assets/prompts/create-instructions.prompt.md">
Prompt instructions file:
--
Related skill: `agent-customization`. Load and follow **instructions.md** for template and principles.

Guide the user to create an instructions file.

## Extract from Conversation
First, review the conversation history. If the user has been correcting the agent's output or asking for specific patterns (e.g., "always use X", "never do Y", "follow this style"), generalize that into a persistent instruction. Extract:
- Corrections or preferences mentioned during the conversation
- Coding patterns the user enforced or requested
- Project-specific conventions referenced

## Clarify if Needed
If no clear rule emerges from the conversation, clarify:
- Should this apply everywhere or only to specific files?
- Which technologies or file types are affected?
- Is this a hard rule or a preference?

Explore the codebase using subagents if you need more context.

## Iterate
1. Draft the instruction and save it.
2. Identify the most ambiguous or weak parts and ask about those.
3. Once finalized, summarize what the instruction enforces, suggest example prompts to see it in action, and propose related customizations to create next.

Remember to follow the `agent-customization` guidelines to create highly effective instructions.

</attachment>
Follow instructions in [create-instructions.prompt.md](file:///home/bap/.vscode/extensions/github.copilot-chat-0.38.2/assets/prompts/create-instructions.prompt.md).
</user_query>

## Shell Configuration (Fish/Zsh)

toolCallRounds.response:
I've created a draft `copilot-instructions.md` file in the `.github/` folder of your dotfiles workspace, based on the scope you specified (fish/zsh and Hyprland configurations). The instructions are currently general guidelines for best practices in those areas, plus some general dotfiles advice.

To make this more effective, the draft has some ambiguous or weak parts:
- The shell and Hyprland rules are broad and could be more specific (e.g., particular syntax requirements, common pitfalls, or preferred patterns).
- The general practices section assumes standard dotfiles conventions but might not cover your specific workflow.

Could you provide more details on what specific rules or behaviors you'd like enforced? For example:
- For fish/zsh: Any particular functions, plugins, or syntax patterns to always use or avoid?
- For Hyprland: Specific keybinding formats, animation settings, or window rules to follow?
- Any other preferences for the workspace?

Once we refine this, I can finalize the instructions, summarize what they enforce, suggest example prompts to test them, and propose related customizations (like file-specific instructions or skills).

children.text: description: '**WORKFLOW SKILL** — Create, update, review, fix, or debug VS Code agent customization files (.instructions.md, .prompt.md, .agent.md, SKILL.md, copilot-instructions.md, AGENTS.md). USE FOR: saving coding preferences; troubleshooting why instructions/skills/agents are ignored or not invoked; configuring applyTo patterns; defining tool restrictions; creating custom agent modes or specialized workflows; packaging domain knowledge; fixing YAML frontmatter syntax. DO NOT USE FOR: general coding questions (use default agent); runtime debugging or error diagnosis; MCP server configuration (use MCP docs directly); VS Code extension development. INVOKES: file system tools (read/write customization files), ask-questions tool (interview user for requirements), subagents for codebase exploration. FOR SINGLE OPERATIONS: For quick YAML frontmatter fixes or creating a single file from a known pattern, edit the file directly — no skill needed.'
| Workspace Instructions | Always-on, applies everywhere in the project |
| File Instructions | Explicit via `applyTo` patterns, or on-demand via `description` |
| MCP | Integrates external systems, APIs, or data |
| Hooks | Deterministic shell commands at agent lifecycle points (block tools, auto-format, inject context) |
| Custom Agents | Subagents for context isolation, or multi-stage workflows with tool restrictions |
| Prompts | Single focused task with parameterized inputs |
| Skills | On-demand workflow with bundled assets (scripts/templates) |

Consult the reference docs for templates, domain examples, advanced frontmatter options, asset organization, anti-patterns, and creation checklists. If the references are not enough, load the official documentation links for each primitive.
Customizations roam with user's settings sync
If you need to explore or validate patterns in the codebase, use a read-only subagent. If the ask-questions tool is available, use it to interview the user and clarify requirements.
Follow these steps when creating any customization file.
Ask the user where they want the customization:
Use the Decision Flow above to select the appropriate file type based on the user's need.
Create the file directly at the appropriate path:
- Use the location tables in each reference file
- Add the body content following the templates
- Confirm the file is in the correct location
- Check that `description` is present and meaningful
**Instructions vs Skill?** Does this apply to *most* work, or *specific* tasks? Most → Instructions. Specific → Skill.
**Skill vs Prompt?** Both appear as slash commands in chat (type `/`). Multi-step workflow with bundled assets → Skill. Single focused task with inputs → Prompt.
**Skill vs Custom Agent?** Same capabilities for all steps → Skill. Need context isolation (subagent returns single output) or different tool restrictions per stage → Custom Agent.

**Hooks vs Instructions?** Instructions *guide* agent behavior (non-deterministic). Hooks *enforce* behavior via shell commands at lifecycle events like `PreToolUse` or `PostToolUse` — they can block operations, require approval, or run formatters deterministically. See [hooks reference](./references/hooks.md).

**Description is the discovery surface.** The `description` field is how the agent decides whether to load a skill, instruction, or agent. If trigger phrases aren't IN the description, the agent won't find it. Use the "Use when..." pattern with specific keywords.

**YAML frontmatter silent failures.** Unescaped colons in values, tabs instead of spaces, `name` that doesn't match folder name — all cause silent failures with no error message. Always quote descriptions that contain colons: `description: "Use when: doing X"`.

**`applyTo: "**"` burns context.** This means "always included for every file request" — it loads the instruction into the context window on every interaction, even when irrelevant. Use specific globs (`**/*.py`, `src/api/**`) unless the instruction truly applies to all files.

content.value:
Based on my exploration of the dotfiles workspace, here's a comprehensive summary:

## **Workspace Purpose**
This is a **Linux desktop configuration repository** for a fully customized Wayland-based development environment running on **CachyOS** (Arch-based distribution). The dotfiles follow the **XDG Base Directory Specification** with `.config/` subdirectories for each application.

---

## **Core Architecture**

### **Display & Desktop Environment**
- **Window Manager**: **Hyprland** (Wayland compositor)
- **Session Manager**: UWSM (Universal Wayland Session Manager) - auto-started at TTY1
- **Theme Manager**: **Omarchy** (custom theming system) - manages themes across multiple applications
- **Desktop**: CachyOS (distribution-specific configurations)

### **Shells & Terminals**
- **Primary Shell Options**:
  - **Fish Shell** - Advanced configuration with completions, functions, plugins, and custom themes
  - **Zsh Shell** - Using Oh-My-Zsh framework with `xiong-chiamiov-plus` theme
  - Plugins: `git`, `dnf`, `zsh-autosuggestions`, `zsh-syntax-highlighting`

- **Terminal Emulator**: **Alacritty**
  - Uses JetBrainsMono Nerd Font (size 14)
  - Integrates Omarchy theme system for consistent styling
  - Configured with 14px padding and borderless decorations

### **Code Editors**
- **Neovim**: LazyVim starter configuration (modern Lua-based setup)
  - Uses lazy package manager (`lazy-lock.json`)
  - Plugin system with custom configuration in `lua/` directory
  - Includes LSP support and extensive tooling

- **VS Code**: Full configuration stored (user settings, extensions, MCP servers)

---

## **Tools & Utilities**

| Category | Tools |
|----------|-------|
| **System Monitoring** | btop (system monitor) |
| **Media/Audio** | cava (audio visualization with Omarchy theming) |
| **Input Methods** | fcitx, fcitx5 (CJK language support) |
| **Font Config** | Custom fontconfig (fonts.conf) |
| **Application Menu** | elephant + walker (with Lua-based custom menus) |
| **System Info** | fastfetch (with Omarchy version/theme display) |
| **PDF Viewer** | evince (with custom settings) |
| **Autostart Apps** | walker (launcher), arch-update-tray |

---

## **Configuration Patterns & Structure**

### **Directory Organization**
```
dotfiles/
├── fish/                 # Fish shell config
├── zsh/                  # Zsh config (.zshrc)
├── nvim/                 # Neovim (LazyVim)
├── alacritty/            # Terminal emulator
├── btop/                 # System monitor theme
├── cava/                 # Audio visualizer
├── fcitx/ & fcitx5/      # Input methods
├── elephant/             # Menu system + Omarchy integrations
├── fastfetch/            # System info display
├── fontconfig/           # Font configuration
├── dconf/                # GNOME dconf settings (user profiles)
├── evince/               # PDF viewer settings
├── cachyos/              # CachyOS-specific configs
├── Code/                 # VS Code configuration
├── environment.d/        # Environment variables
└── autostart/            # Desktop autostart entries
```

### **Theme Integration Pattern**
The setup heavily integrates **Omarchy** theming:
- Terminal (Alacritty) imports theme from `~/.config/omarchy/current/theme/alacritty.toml`
- Cava uses `theme = 'omarchy'`
- Elephant includes Lua menus for dynamic Omarchy theme switching
- Fastfetch displays current Omarchy version and theme

---

## **Key Technologies & Frameworks**
- **Wayland Protocol** (modern display server)
- **Hyprland** (Wayland compositor with animations, workspaces, window rules)
- **LazyVim** (modern Neovim distribution)
- **Oh-My-Zsh** (Zsh plugin framework)
- **XDG Base Directory Spec** (standard Linux config locations)
- **TOML/JSON/Lua** configuration formats

---

## **No Central README**
The workspace has **no top-level README or main documentation file** - configuration is organized by tool/application rather than having a central guide. Individual components (like Neovim) have their own README entries, but the overall setup documentation is implicit in the structure.

This appears to be a **highly customized, theme-focused development setup** emphasizing visual consistency, modern technologies (Wayland, Hyprland), and internationalization (fcitx5) across all system components.

edits.text: description: Workspace instructions for dotfiles repository, focusing on fish/zsh shell configs and Hyprland window manager configs.
## Shell Configuration (Fish/Zsh)
When editing or creating fish or zsh configuration files:
- Ensure proper shell syntax and follow best practices for shell scripting.
- Consider cross-compatibility if configurations need to work in both fish and zsh environments.
- Use appropriate quoting and escaping for variables and commands.
## Hyprland Configuration
When working with Hyprland configuration files:
- Be mindful of Hyprland's configuration syntax, including window rules, keybindings, and animations.
- Consider performance implications when adding complex animations or rules.
- Ensure configurations align with Wayland standards.
## General Dotfiles Practices
- Follow XDG Base Directory Specification for configuration file locations.
- Use the omarchy skill for theming-related customizations in applications like alacritty, cava, and other themed components.
- Maintain consistency across related configurations for a cohesive desktop experience.

v.value: I've created a draft `copilot-instructions.md` file in the

folder of your dotfiles workspace, based on the scope you specified (fish/zsh and Hyprland configurations). The instructions are currently general guidelines for best practices in those areas, plus some general dotfiles advice.

To make this more effective, the draft has some ambiguous or weak parts:
- The shell and Hyprland rules are broad and could be more specific (e.g., particular syntax requirements, common pitfalls, or preferred patterns).
- The general practices section assumes standard dotfiles conventions but might not cover your specific workflow.

Could you provide more details on what specific rules or behaviors you'd like enforced? For example:
- For fish/zsh: Any particular functions, plugins, or syntax patterns to always use or avoid?
- For Hyprland: Specific keybinding formats, animation settings, or window rules to follow?
- Any other preferences for the workspace?

Once we refine this, I can finalize the instructions, summarize what they enforce, suggest example prompts to test them, and propose related customizations (like file-specific instructions or skills).

agent.description: Edit files in your workspace in agent mode
agent.name: agent

slashCommands.name: compact
slashCommands.description: Free up context by compacting the conversation history. Optionally include extra instructions for compaction.

slashCommands.name: explain
slashCommands.description: Explain how the code in your active editor works

slashCommands.name: tests
slashCommands.description: Generate unit tests for the selected code

disambiguation.description: The user wants to generate unit tests.
examples: Generate tests for my selection using pytest.

slashCommands.name: fix
slashCommands.description: Propose a fix for the problems in the selected code
slashCommands.sampleRequest: There is a problem in this code. Rewrite the code to show it with the bug fixed.

slashCommands.name: new
slashCommands.description: Scaffold code for a new file or project in a workspace
slashCommands.sampleRequest: Create a RESTful API server using typescript

disambiguation.description: The user wants to create a complete Visual Studio Code workspace from scratch, such as a new application or a Visual Studio Code extension. Use this category only if the question relates to generating or creating new workspaces in Visual Studio Code. Do not use this category for updating existing code or generating sample code snippets
examples: Create a sample project which uses the fileSystemProvider API.

slashCommands.name: newNotebook
slashCommands.sampleRequest: How do I create a notebook to load data from a csv file?

disambiguation.description: The user wants to create a new Jupyter notebook in Visual Studio Code.
examples: Create a notebook to analyze this CSV file.

slashCommands.name: setupTests
slashCommands.description: Set up tests in your project (Experimental)
slashCommands.sampleRequest: add playwright tests to my project

disambiguation.description: The user wants to configure project test setup, framework, or test runner. The user does not want to fix their existing tests.
examples: Set up tests for this project.

toolCallRounds.response:
Lỗi Git đã được giải quyết. Vấn đề là lệnh commit đang cố gắng mở trình soạn thảo VS Code để nhập thông điệp commit, nhưng script editor gặp lỗi HTTP 500.

Tôi đã commit thành công bằng cách sử dụng tùy chọn `-m` để cung cấp thông điệp commit trực tiếp: "Add workspace instructions for dotfiles".

Commit đã đẩy lên remote repository với 38 tệp thay đổi, bao gồm tệp `.github/copilot-instructions.md` mới được tạo.

Nếu bạn gặp lại lỗi tương tự trong tương lai, bạn có thể:
- Sử dụng `git commit -m "thông điệp"` để commit trực tiếp
- Hoặc cấu hình editor khác: `git config --global core.editor "nano"` hoặc `git config --global core.editor "code --wait"`

Lỗi Git đã được giải quyết. Vấn đề là lệnh commit đang cố gắng mở trình soạn thảo VS Code để nhập thông điệp commit, nhưng script editor gặp lỗi HTTP 500.

Tôi đã commit thành công bằng cách sử dụng tùy chọn `-m` để cung cấp thông điệp commit trực tiếp: "Add workspace instructions for dotfiles".

Commit đã đẩy lên remote repository với 38 tệp thay đổi, bao gồm tệp

mới được tạo.

Nếu bạn gặp lại lỗi tương tự trong tương lai, bạn có thể:
- Sử dụng `git commit -m "thông điệp"` để commit trực tiếp
- Hoặc cấu hình editor khác: `git config --global core.editor "nano"` hoặc `git config --global core.editor "code --wait"`

variables.name: prompt:instructionsList
variables.value:
<skills>
Here is a list of skills that contain domain specific knowledge on a variety of topics.
Each skill comes with a description of the topic and a file path that contains the detailed instructions.
When a user asks you to perform a task that falls within the domain of a skill, use the #tool:readFile tool to acquire the full instructions from the file URI.
<skill>
<name>omarchy</name>
<description>REQUIRED for end-user customization of Linux desktop, window manager, or system config. Use when editing ~/.config/hypr/, ~/.config/waybar/, ~/.config/walker/, ~/.config/alacritty/, ~/.config/kitty/, ~/.config/ghostty/, ~/.config/mako/, or ~/.config/omarchy/. Triggers: Hyprland, window rules, animations, keybindings, monitors, gaps, borders, blur, opacity, waybar, walker, terminal config, themes, wallpaper, night light, idle, lock screen, screenshots, layer rules, workspace settings, display config, and user-facing omarchy commands. Excludes Omarchy source development in ~/.local/share/omarchy/ and omarchy-dev-* workflows.
</description>
<file>/home/bap/.claude/skills/omarchy/SKILL.md</file>
</skill>
<skill>
<name>agent-customization</name>
<description>**WORKFLOW SKILL** — Create, update, review, fix, or debug VS Code agent customization files (.instructions.md, .prompt.md, .agent.md, SKILL.md, copilot-instructions.md, AGENTS.md). USE FOR: saving coding preferences; troubleshooting why instructions/skills/agents are ignored or not invoked; configuring applyTo patterns; defining tool restrictions; creating custom agent modes or specialized workflows; packaging domain knowledge; fixing YAML frontmatter syntax. DO NOT USE FOR: general coding questions (use default agent); runtime debugging or error diagnosis; MCP server configuration (use MCP docs directly); VS Code extension development. INVOKES: file system tools (read/write customization files), ask-questions tool (interview user for requirements), subagents for codebase exploration. FOR SINGLE OPERATIONS: For quick YAML frontmatter fixes or creating a single file from a known pattern, edit the file directly — no skill needed.</description>
<file>copilot-skill:/agent-customization/SKILL.md</file>
</skill>
</skills>


<agents>
Here is a list of agents that can be used when running a subagent.
Each agent has optionally a description with the agent's purpose and expertise. When asked to run a subagent, choose the most appropriate agent from this list.
Use the #tool:runSubagent tool with the agent name to run the subagent.
<agent>
<name>Explore</name>
<description>Fast read-only codebase exploration and Q&A subagent. Prefer over manually chaining multiple search and file-reading operations to avoid cluttering the main conversation. Safe to call in parallel. Specify thoroughness: quick, medium, or thorough.</description>
<argumentHint>Describe WHAT you're looking for and desired thoroughness (quick/medium/thorough)</argumentHint>
</agent>
</agents>
